data.title: White House to widen its AI-robotics lens; see top sector names by market cap
data.description:
The Trump administration is intensifying its focus on robotics as a part of its broader initiative to accelerate the development of artificial intelligence, Politico reported Wednesday.

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick is actively engaging with leaders of the robotics industry and is considering an executive order to advance this sector’s growth, the report said. This push aligns with efforts to compete with China, which leads the world in industrial robotics deployment with 1.8 million robots—four times the U.S. total.

Here are the top robotic and AI-related stocks ranked by their market cap: 

  • Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) – $350.42B
  • Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI) – $133.66B
  • Fanuc Corporation (OTCPK:FANUY) – $32.13B
  • UiPath Inc. (PATH) – $7.59B
  • ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding Limited (ZK) – $6.84B
  • BigBear.ai Holdings, Inc. (BBAI) – $2.53B
  • Tuya Inc. (TUYA) – $1.36B
  • Radware Ltd. (RDWR) – $1.01B
  • Information Services Group, Inc. (III) – $262.88M

data.title: GitLab gets down following Q3 results; analysts point to Fed, SMB softness
data.description:
GitLab (GTLB) shares sank 15% by early market action on Wednesday following third-quarter results that once again surpassed expectations, as analysts point to a slowdown from the public sector due to the U.S. federal government shutdown and pressure among small-to-medium sized businesses.

"GTLB noted benefit driven by downstream effects of more AI-driven code generation, but headwinds from ongoing weakness in SMB (8% ARR) and lingering effects from the U.S. federal shutdown (will also impact 4Q)," said Wells Fargo analysts Ryan MacWilliams and Chris Brazeau in an investor note. "Customer adds saw pressure, with 956 net new total customers (lowest in the past 4 years). RPO and cRPO decelerated to 27% y/y and 28% y/y, respectively, which remain at elevated growth levels but leave investors questioning where the rev. guide starts next year."

Wells Fargo retained its Equal Weight rating but lowered its price target to $45 from $50.

Similarly, Mizuho maintained its Neutral rating and lowered its price target to $47 from $52.

"Broadly, while we view GTLB's platform as strategically important within DevSecOps, we continue to see ongoing risks: (1) uncertainty over whether AI will erode its developer-heavy seat model, (2) intensifying competition from GitHub and AI-native startups, (3) potentially weaker monetization than management implies, particularly for the Premium SKU, and (4) uncertain benefits from the new GTM strategy," said Mizuho analyst Gregg Moskowitz in a note.

Meanwhile, Cantor was more upbeat, reiterating its Overweight rating and $60 price target.

"GitLab Ultimate continues to drive platform adoption and success, accounting for 54% of total ARR, with seven of the firm's top ten deals in the quarter being Ultimate," said Cantor analysts Jonathan Ruykhaver and Ben Mitchell in a note. "Customers are drawn to its security and compliance features, and management highlights a payback period of less than six months and an ROI of 480% over three years."

GitLab Ultimate is the most advanced tier of the company's DevSecOps platform. It offers a comprehensive set of features for enterprises, particularly those with advanced security, compliance and planning needs.

Cantor also pointed to improved margin gains, as third quarter operating margins increased to 17.9% compared to 13.2% one year ago. Free cash flow margins also rose to 11.1%, up from 4.9% one year prior.

Finally, RBC maintained its Outperform rating and $58 price target.

"Management noted steady progress on first-order business vs. expansions, though it is not where it needs to be with room for improvement," said RBC analysts, led by Matthew Hedberg, in a Wednesday note. "They also noted Gitlab Duo Agent Platform is on schedule to go GA in the coming weeks, which is a positive. This will help transition the business model from purely seat-based to a hybrid seat/consumption model."

Competitor JFrog (FROG) was down 1%, and Microsoft (MSFT), which offers the similar GitHub had declined 2.9%.

data.title: U.S. stocks mixed after jobs data drags sentiment, Microsoft slashes AI sales quotas
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Wednesday, after the latest private sector jobs data fell, while investors assessed Microsoft's (MSFT) software sales quotas tied to AI being cut.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last flat in late morning trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.2%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.3%.

The U.S. private sector employment fell by 32K in November, compared with the 5K consensus and October's 47K (revised from 42K), according to data released by ADP on Wednesday.

“It’s a K-shaped economy and labor market as the Nov ADP private payroll estimate indicated a 32K decline in hiring. For the past 6 out of 7 months small businesses have seen losses and were down -120K. These are firms without adequate capital depth and are likely experiencing stress in adjusting to higher prices and taxes such as tariffs,” said Joseph Brusuelas, RSM U.S. principal and chief economist.

“However, mid-size businesses added 51K and large firms added 39K as those businesses have exposure to the upper spur of the K that is doing quite well right now,” Brusuelas added.

Also, the S&P Global U.S. Composite PMI came in softer than expected at 54.2 in November, compared to October's 54.6, while the U.S. ISM Services Index edged up in November, beating consensus.

Microsoft (MSFT) shares were -2.9% after The Information reported that the tech giant is cutting software sales quotas tied to artificial intelligence.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD), on the other hand, continued its recovery, now over $92,000.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 3 basis points to 4.07%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 2 basis points to 3.50%.

On the earnings side, Salesforce (CRM) and Snowflake (SNOW), among others, will report their results.

In other economic news, the U.S. import and export prices were unchanged month-over-month in September.

data.title: ISM Services PMI rises more than expected in November
data.description: The U.S. ISM Services Index edged up to 52.6 in November from 52.4 in October, beating the 52.0 consensus, according to data released by the Institute for Supply Management on Wednesday.

data.title: DocuSign Q3 earnings on deck: What to expect
data.description:
DocuSign (DOCU) is set to post third quarter results on Thursday, after markets close.

Wall Street expects the San Francisco-based company to post EPS of $0.91 on revenue of $807.42 million, implying a rise of 7% during the quarter.

The e-signature provider’s artificial intelligence offerings have impressed its customer base. Earlier in September, the company posted second quarter results and an outlook that topped estimates. Docusign in Q2 also launched new AI-powered IAM, Intelligent Agreement Management, capabilities to help customers.

Investors will keep an eye on billings growth as well as outlook.

Over the last two years, DocuSign has beaten both revenue and EPS estimates 100% of the time. Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen 13 upward revisions and four downward revisions, while revenue estimates have seen 18 upward revisions versus no downward move.

“I believe the company has substantial tailwinds that could drive further growth. If DOCU can experience a continuation in both top and bottom line growth, I believe the market may reprice the stock at a higher premium multiple,” noted a recent Seeking Alpha analysis.

Seeking Alpha analysts are bullish and rated the stock a Buy. In contrast, both Wall Street and Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings consider it a Hold.

The stock has lost over 20% so far this year, underperforming the 16% rise in the broader S&P 500 index.

data.title: LME copper hits another record high on supply concerns
data.description:
Copper futures on the London Metal Exchange climbed to new record highs on Wednesday, driven by prospects of a December interest rate cut by the Federal Reserve, concerns over global supply, and a weaker U.S. dollar.

The LME's global benchmark price for copper is up more than 30% this year, but U.S. futures have rallied even more, with investors betting the Trump administration will announce tariffs on primary forms of the metal.

Traders have been ramping up shipments to the U.S. amid fears of potential future tariffs, tightening supply in other regions; according to ANZ Research, several commodity traders have approached Chilean producers to lock in U.S. annual supply deals for 2026, with some paying more than $500/ton over benchmark LME prices.

Trump unveiled plans for copper tariffs in February, which rocked the global copper market and drove U.S. copper imports to record highs, but in July, he said he would limit the levies to value-added copper products, while reviewing whether to push ahead with tariffs on commodity-grade forms of the metal.

The decision has caused copper traders to again ramp up shipments to U.S. ports as U.S. futures surge, and producers have announced they will charge record premiums to supply customers in Europe and Asia next year, with buyers in effect compensating them for the additional profits they could make selling to the U.S.

Futures on the London Metal Exchange (HG1:COM) recently traded +1.8% to $11,348/metric ton on Wednesday, after hitting an intraday peak of $11,434.50/ton.

ETFs: (CPER), (COPX), (OTC:JJCTF)

Potentially relevant stocks include Freeport McMoRan (FCX), Southern Copper (SCCO), Ero Copper (ERO), Hudbay Minerals (HBM), Teck Resources (TECK), Taseko Mines (TGB), Rio Tinto (RIO), BHP (BHP), Antofagasta (OTC:ANFGF), Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY).

data.title: Waymo takes on ice and snow as it expands to Baltimore, Philadelphia, Pittsburgh, and St. Louis
data.description:
Waymo (WAYMO) announced on Wednesday that it is beginning manual testing of its autonomous vehicles in four new U.S. cities.

The autonomous vehicle company's expansion into Baltimore, Philadelphia, Pittsburgh, and St. Louis is notable because it means the service is extending its footprint into colder-weather, rust-belt-style markets beyond its existing Sun Belt and West Coast strongholds. As usual, the Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGLE) company is starting with human-driven mapping and data collection in the new cities, with the goal of progressing to fully autonomous testing and eventually commercial robotaxi service once it clears safety, regulatory, and operational milestones.

The testing phase involves Waymo vehicles operating in regular traffic with safety drivers behind the wheel as they learn local road geometry, traffic patterns, and weather conditions in Baltimore, Philadelphia, Pittsburgh, and St. Louis. The challenging weather conditions in the test cities mark an important step in proving that the Waymo Driver can handle snow, ice, and complex East Coast and Midwestern urban layouts.

Waymo (WAYMO) has already delivered over 250,000 paid trips per week in existing robotaxi markets such as Phoenix, the San Francisco Bay Area, Los Angeles, Atlanta, and Austin. Waymo (WAYMO) management has publicly targeted reaching roughly one million robotaxi trips per week by the end of 2026.

data.title: Microchip rises after revised Q3 outlook surpasses estimates
data.description:
Shares of Microchip Technology (MCHP) rose about 7% on Wednesday after the company's revised revenue and EPS outlook for the third quarter of fiscal 2026, ending Dec. 31, came above estimates.

The company now expects its net sales and EPS to be at the high end of its previously provided guidance, representing sequential growth of roughly 1% that exceeds the midpoint of its prior guidance, which anticipated a sequential decline in net sales.

While reporting its second quarter results on Nov. 6, Microchip provided its third quarter outlook, forecasting it to be between $1.109B and $1.149B (midpoint at $1.129B). The consensus revenue estimate is $1.14B.

The company said the revised revenue guidance would represent 12% year-over-year growth.

In addition, Microchip now expects non-GAAP EPS to be about $0.40 versus the prior estimate range of $0.34 and $0.40 (midpoint at $0.37). The new guidance surpasses the consensus EPS estimate of $0.38.

"With two months of the quarter behind us, our business is performing better than we expected at the time of our November 6, 2025 earnings conference call. Our bookings activity has remained strong through November, with backlog filling in better than expected in the current quarter and growing nicely into the March 2026 quarter," said Microchip's President and CEO Steve Sanghi.

Related stocks: NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) jumped about 3%, while Texas Instruments (TXN) and STMicroelectronics (STM) each climbed nearly 2%. Marvell Technology (MRVL) surged about 7%, while ON Semiconductor (ON) soared around 4%. Analog Devices (ADI) was up nearly 1%.
